_comment: Per-chart narratives. CI (build_prompt.py -> GitHub Models -> write_narrative.py) overwrites these from live numbers; this committed copy is the fallback so the page always reads well. source = 'fallback' | 'github-models'.

notes.shape: The yield curve plots Treasury yields across maturities, typically from 1 month to 30 years, and an upward slope is considered normal as longer maturities generally carry higher yields reflecting risks over time. An inverted yield curve, where short-term yields exceed long-term yields, has historically preceded every modern US recession by 6-18 months, although it may un-invert shortly before recession onset. Currently, the 10-year Treasury yield stands at 4.44% (June 2026 maturity). Compared to 2023, when the curve was inverted with 10Y-2Y spreads below 0.0 points, the 2s10s spread has re-steepened to 0.31 points, indicating a positive slope now.
notes.signal: The 2s10s spread measures the difference between the 10-year and 2-year Treasury yields and is a widely followed recession indicator. Its previous inversion in the 2022-2024 period signaled elevated recession risk, but the current positive 0.31-point spread reflects an un-inversion, reducing the immediacy of that signal. However, the New York Fed's recession probability remains at 31%, suggesting material, though not definitive, recession risk. It is important to note that the yield curve often un-inverts shortly before a recession begins, which can temporarily mute this signal.
notes.now: At present, the yield curve indicates a normalized upward slope after a prolonged inversion period, while the positive 2s10s spread and a 31% recession probability point to ongoing risks without confirming imminent recession. This configuration suggests caution and attentiveness to other economic indicators for further clarity.
